|Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba {{jalink|Koaza|Q5327509|aza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 == Overview == This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 == Mifune Shrine == Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” == Mumino Shrine == Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. == History == By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. == Access == ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. == Notes == {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == Related articles == * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} == External links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} ==End Japanese== [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|御船神社 (多気町)|version=107195393|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba {{jalink|Koaza|Q5327509|aza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 == Overview == This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 == Mifune Shrine == Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” == Mumino Shrine == Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. == History == By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. == Access == ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. == Notes == {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == Related articles == * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} == External links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} ==End Japanese== [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|御船神社 (多気町)|version=107195393|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 == Overview == This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 == Mifune Shrine == Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” == Mumino Shrine == Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. == History == By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. == Access == ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. == Notes == {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} == References == * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == Related articles == * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} == External links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

[[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|御船神社 (多気町)|version=107195393|comment=Imported full ja history}}== Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba {{jalink|Koaza|Q5327509|aza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]]

(2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]]

[[Category:Autogenerated pages with jawiki interwikis, possibly accidentally overwritten]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Sessha]] [[Category:Massha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[JA:御船神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17214615}} [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|御船神社 (多気町)|version=107195393|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Pages]] == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba {{jalink|Koaza|Q5327509|aza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

(多気町)|version=107195393|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Pages]] == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}{{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}} Toba {{jalink|Koaza|Q5327509|aza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: Subsidiary shrine of the {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: Auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{jalink|Kannamesai|Q11255045}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} **Mifune Shrine** (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}} of the {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} {{jalink|Kōtaijingū|Q11581011}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes **Mumino Shrine** (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{jalink|water deities|Q3137449|river gods}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (pronounced *Toba* or *Doba*), {{jalink|Taki Town|Q1203963}}, {{jalink|Taki District|Q1042360}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 378 The local {{jalink|place name|Q7884789}} “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the tradition that the boat of {{jalink|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|Q4388732}} once stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p. 55 The {{jalink|shrine buildings}} are constructed in the {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} style and are surrounded by a {{jalink|tamagaki|Q3078312}} fence. There is no {{jalink|offering box|Q988479}}. A {{jalink|stone lantern|Q762277}} stands at the entrance to the shrine grounds. The total {{jalink|area|Q11500}} of the grounds is 975 {{jalink|tsubo|Q706688}} (approximately 3,223 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 9–11 It is said to be a shrine established by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto when she moored her boat during her journey up the Tokida River (Outer Tokida River). She stopped here because the river narrowed and boats could no longer pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat upon which {{jalink|Amaterasu||Amaterasu Ōmikami}} rode. Sonoda Moriyoshi suggested that after the transfer of the deity, the boat container (*mifunashiro*) holding the sacred casket (*mihishiro*) that enshrined the {{jalink|divine body|Q327532}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami may have been placed at Mifune Shrine. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 68 This deity is regarded either as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates the surrounding fields, or as a guardian deity of waterborne transportation. The same {{jalink|kami (Shinto)|Q524158|deity}} is also enshrined at {{jalink|Kano Shrine|Q17225745}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p. 59 The term “Mikage” carries meanings of {{jalink|spirit|Q9165}} and blessing (“okage”), and according to *Jingū Tenryaku*, it refers to the spirit of a great deity enshrined by the river that supplies water to the {{jalink|sacred fields|Q10855080}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p. 379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nisho Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* records it as “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* as “Ame-no-Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* as “Toriishikusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): pp. 13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is now enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Hall, ed. (1929): p. 14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and are said to be children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, and is said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto because the river’s water was cold. === History === By definition, as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).Ise Bunkasha (2008): p. 22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records the phrase “designated and celebrated by Princess Yamatohime.” Since it appears in this text, the shrine is considered to have existed before {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} 23 ({{jalink|804|Q29998}}). In {{jalink|ancient times|Q41493}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{jalink|Watarai District|Q1131032}}, but in {{jalink|Taika|Q1077930}} 5 ({{jalink|649|Q31117}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Ari-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}} onward, rituals were discontinued, but on {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3, {{jalink|8th month 29th day (lunar calendar)|Q840048}} ({{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138}}: {{jalink|1663|Q7577}} {{jalink|September 30|Q2881}}), the shrine was reestablished at its present site. At that time, remains of moats, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be regarded as the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp. 378–379 {{jalink|Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624}} supported this identification, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that the former site was a local {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha.” In {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 14 ({{jalink|1843|Q7629}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{jalink|Meiji 4|Q11512280}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{jalink|Tokida Station|Q7813584}} on the {{jalink|Sangū Line|Q872023}} of {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}}.Ise Bunkasha, ed. (2008): p. 63 Walk along the {{jalink|Miyagawa irrigation canal|Q11454721}} from the south side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{jalink|kilometers|Q828224|km}}) from {{jalink|Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|Tamaki IC}} on the {{jalink|Ise Expressway|Q6079578}}. There is no {{jalink|parking lot|Q6501349}} nearby. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{jalink|Ise Bunkasha|Q11379080}}, ed., *O-Ise-san 125 Shrines Pilgrimage*, Supplement *Isebito*, Ise Bunkasha, December 23, 2008, 151 pp. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Hall, ed., *History of Ujiyamada City, Vol. 1*, Ujiyamada City Hall, January 20, 1929, 862 pp. * {{jalink|Gakken Publishing}}, *Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine*, Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection vol. 1, supervised by {{jalink|Sonoda Minoru|Q11622218}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pp. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Kaneko Nobuo, *History and Traditions of Southern Ise: Tamaki Town History, Vol. 1*, Mie Prefecture Local History Materials Series No. 61, August 10, 1983, 276 pp. * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed., *Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1*, {{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press, February 28, 1990, 690 pp. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 === Related articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|Q11378973}} * {{jalink|List of the 125 Shrines of Jingū|Q16482760}} === External links === {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area03/area03_06.jpg Mifune Shrine] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0701.html Mifune Shrine (Mifune-jinja), with Mumino Shrine] – Ise Grand Shrine Worship Association {{Jingū 125 Shrines}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]
