== street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町土羽字南出505', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:御船神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17214615}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|qid=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|qid=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki Town|Q1203963|qid=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Toba {{ill|lt=Koaza|Q5327509|qid=Q5327509|lt=aza|1=koaza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kōtaijingū|Q11581011|qid=Q11581011|1=Kōtai Jingū}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before the 23rd year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} ({{ill|lt=804|Q29998|qid=Q29998|1=804}}) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Alternate name= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{ill|lt=Kannamesai|Q11255045|qid=Q11255045|1=Kannamesai Festival}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mifune Shrine''' (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Sessha|Q1071160|qid=Q1071160|lt=subsidiary shrine|1=Setsumatsusha}} of the {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|qid=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} {{ill|lt=Kōtaijingū|Q11581011|qid=Q11581011|1=Kōtai Jingū}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes '''Mumino Shrine''' (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{ill|lt=water deities|Q3137449|qid=Q3137449|lt=river gods|1=Suijin}}.Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p.64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (read as Toba or Doba), {{ill|lt=Taki Town|Q1203963|qid=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|qid=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|qid=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.378 The place name “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the fact that the boat of {{ill|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Q4388732|qid=Q4388732|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p.55 The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}} are constructed in the {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style and are surrounded by a {{ill|lt=tamagaki|Q3078312|qid=Q3078312|1=Sacred fences}} fence. No {{ill|lt=offertory box|Q988479|qid=Q988479|1=Saisen}} is placed. At the entrance to the shrine grounds stands a {{ill|lt=stone lantern|Q762277|qid=Q762277|1=Nightlight}}. The area of the shrine grounds is 975 {{ill|lt=tsubo|Q706688|qid=Q706688|1=Pyeong}} (approximately 3,223 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|qid=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.9–11 It is a shrine established at a place where Yamatohime-no-mikoto moored her boat during her journey upstream along the Tokida River. She stopped here because the river became too narrow for boats to pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat on which {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} rides, and Sonoda Moriyoshi conjectured that the vessel (mifunashiro) containing the sacred container for the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami was placed at Mifune Shrine after the transfer of the deity. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p.68 This deity is regarded as the {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q131278957|qid=Q131278957|1=Guardian deities}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates fields, or as a guardian deity of water transportation. The same {{ill|lt=kami (Shinto)|Q524158|qid=Q524158|lt=deity|1=Kami}} is also enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kano Shrine|Q17225745|qid=Q17225745|1=Kano Shrine}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p.59 “Mikage” carries meanings of {{ill|lt=spirit|Q9165|qid=Q9165|1=Soul}} and blessing (“okage”), and *Jingū Tenryaku* explains it as the spirit of the great deity enshrined beside the river that draws water to the {{ill|lt=sacred rice fields|Q10855080|qid=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nishō Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* gives “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* gives “Ame no Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* gives “Toriishi Kusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p.14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-mikoto because the river water was cold. === History === By definition as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}).Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records “Yamatohime Naishinnō Sadame-iwai.” Since it is mentioned in the *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō*, it is considered to have existed before the 23rd year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} ({{ill|lt=804|Q29998|qid=Q29998|1=804}}). In {{ill|lt=ancient times|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{ill|lt=Watarai District|Q1131032|qid=Q1131032|1=Watarai District}}, but in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|qid=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} ({{ill|lt=649|Q31117|qid=Q31117|1=649}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Uni-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward, rituals ceased, but the shrine was revived at its present location on the 29th day of the 8th month of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} ({{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q12138|qid=Q12138|1=Gregorian calendar}}: September 30, {{ill|lt=1663|Q7577|qid=Q7577|1=1663}}). At that time, traces of a {{ill|lt=moat|Q88480|qid=Q88480|1=Moat}}, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be considered the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp.378–379 {{ill|lt=Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624|qid=Q6537624|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao}} supported this theory, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that a {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|qid=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha” by locals, was the former site. In the 14th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|qid=Q7629|1=1843}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|qid=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}} ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{ill|lt=Tokida Station|Q7813584|qid=Q7813584|1=Tokida Station}} on the {{ill|lt=JR|Q513679|qid=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sangū Line|Q872023|qid=Q872023|1=Sangū Line}}.Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p.63 Walk along the {{ill|lt=Miyagawa Irrigation Canal|Q11454721|qid=Q11454721|1=Miyagawa Irrigation Canal}} from the front (south) side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground visible on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{ill|lt=kilometers|Q828224|qid=Q828224|lt=km|1=Kilometre}}) from {{ill|lt=Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|qid=Q11572465|lt=Tamaki IC|1=Tamaki interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Ise Expressway|Q6079578|qid=Q6079578|1=Ise Expressway}}. There is no {{ill|lt=parking lot|Q6501349|qid=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} nearby. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{ill|lt=Ise Bunka-sha|Q11379080|qid=Q11379080|1=Ise == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町土羽字南出505', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:御船神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17214615}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name= Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine |Image= [[File:Mifune-jinja-Mumino-jinja.jpg|280px]] |Location= {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|qid=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|qid=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki Town|Q1203963|qid=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Toba {{ill|lt=Koaza|Q5327509|qid=Q5327509|lt=aza|1=koaza}} Minamide 505 |Coordinates= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Mifune Shrine: Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami
Mumino Shrine: Samukawahiko no Mikoto, Samukawahime no Mikoto |Shrine rank= Mifune Shrine: subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kōtaijingū|Q11581011|qid=Q11581011|1=Kōtai Jingū}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (minor)
Mumino Shrine: auxiliary shrine of the Kōtaijingū |Founded= Before the 23rd year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} ({{ill|lt=804|Q29998|qid=Q29998|1=804}}) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Alternate name= |Pilgrimage sites= |Annual festival= |Rituals= {{ill|lt=Kannamesai|Q11255045|qid=Q11255045|1=Kannamesai Festival}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Mifune Shrine /
Mumino Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mifune Shrine''' (みふねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Sessha|Q1071160|qid=Q1071160|lt=subsidiary shrine|1=Setsumatsusha}} of the {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|qid=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} {{ill|lt=Kōtaijingū|Q11581011|qid=Q11581011|1=Kōtai Jingū}} (Inner Shrine). This article also describes '''Mumino Shrine''' (むみのじんじゃ), which is enshrined at the same site as Mifune Shrine. Both are shrines that enshrine {{ill|lt=water deities|Q3137449|qid=Q3137449|lt=river gods|1=Suijin}}.Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p.64 === Overview === This section describes matters common to both shrines. They are located in Toba (read as Toba or Doba), {{ill|lt=Taki Town|Q1203963|qid=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|qid=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|qid=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.378 The place name “Toba” means “stopping place,” and is said to derive from the fact that the boat of {{ill|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Q4388732|qid=Q4388732|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} stopped there.Kaneko (1983): p.55 The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}} are constructed in the {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style and are surrounded by a {{ill|lt=tamagaki|Q3078312|qid=Q3078312|1=Sacred fences}} fence. No {{ill|lt=offertory box|Q988479|qid=Q988479|1=Saisen}} is placed. At the entrance to the shrine grounds stands a {{ill|lt=stone lantern|Q762277|qid=Q762277|1=Nightlight}}. The area of the shrine grounds is 975 {{ill|lt=tsubo|Q706688|qid=Q706688|1=Pyeong}} (approximately 3,223 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|qid=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}).Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.380 === Mifune Shrine === Mifune Shrine ranks 24th among the 27 subsidiary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.9–11 It is a shrine established at a place where Yamatohime-no-mikoto moored her boat during her journey upstream along the Tokida River. She stopped here because the river became too narrow for boats to pass. In the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, it is recorded as “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine.” “Ōkami no Mifune” is thought to mean the boat on which {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} rides, and Sonoda Moriyoshi conjectured that the vessel (mifunashiro) containing the sacred container for the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}} of Amaterasu Ōmikami was placed at Mifune Shrine after the transfer of the deity. The enshrined deity is Ōkami no Mikagekawa no Kami.Gakken Publishing (2013): p.68 This deity is regarded as the {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q131278957|qid=Q131278957|1=Guardian deities}} of the Tokida River, which irrigates fields, or as a guardian deity of water transportation. The same {{ill|lt=kami (Shinto)|Q524158|qid=Q524158|lt=deity|1=Kami}} is also enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kano Shrine|Q17225745|qid=Q17225745|1=Kano Shrine}}, another subsidiary shrine of the Inner Shrine.Kaneko (1983): p.59 “Mikage” carries meanings of {{ill|lt=spirit|Q9165|qid=Q9165|1=Soul}} and blessing (“okage”), and *Jingū Tenryaku* explains it as the spirit of the great deity enshrined beside the river that draws water to the {{ill|lt=sacred rice fields|Q10855080|qid=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}}.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): p.379 There are alternative theories regarding the enshrined deity: *Ise Nishō Daijingū Shinmei Hisho* gives “Ōkami no Mifune no Kami,” *Engishiki Jinmyōchō Hian-shū* gives “Ame no Torifune no Kami,” and *Ise no Kuni Jinmyōchō Kōshō* gives “Toriishi Kusufune no Kami.” === Mumino Shrine === Mumino Shrine ranks 15th among the 16 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13–14 Its shrine building was discontinued, and it is enshrined together with Mifune Shrine.Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): p.14 The enshrined deities are Samukawahiko no Mikoto and Samukawahime no Mikoto. They are guardian deities of the Tokida River and children of Ōminakami no Mikoto. “Samukawa,” the name of the deities, is an alternate name for the Tokida River, said to have been given by Yamatohime-no-mikoto because the river water was cold. === History === By definition as a subsidiary shrine of Ise Grand Shrine, Mifune Shrine must have been founded before the compilation of the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō*, that is, before the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}).Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22 The *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō* records “Yamatohime Naishinnō Sadame-iwai.” Since it is mentioned in the *Kōtaijingū Gishikichō*, it is considered to have existed before the 23rd year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} ({{ill|lt=804|Q29998|qid=Q29998|1=804}}). In {{ill|lt=ancient times|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}}, Toba belonged to Tanabe-go of {{ill|lt=Watarai District|Q1131032|qid=Q1131032|1=Watarai District}}, but in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|qid=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} ({{ill|lt=649|Q31117|qid=Q31117|1=649}}), Taki District was separated from Watarai District, and the area came to belong to Uni-go of Taki District. Because of this history, the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* lists “Ōkami no Mifune Shrine” under Watarai District. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward, rituals ceased, but the shrine was revived at its present location on the 29th day of the 8th month of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} ({{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q12138|qid=Q12138|1=Gregorian calendar}}: September 30, {{ill|lt=1663|Q7577|qid=Q7577|1=1663}}). At that time, traces of a {{ill|lt=moat|Q88480|qid=Q88480|1=Moat}}, old post holes, and decayed wood were discovered, leading the site to be considered the former shrine location.Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. (1990): pp.378–379 {{ill|lt=Mikanagi Kiyonao|Q6537624|qid=Q6537624|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao}} supported this theory, while Nakagawa Tsunemasa argued that a {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|qid=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} shrine in a neighboring village, colloquially called “Mifune-sha” by locals, was the former site. In the 14th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|qid=Q7629|1=1843}}), a stone lantern was installed at the shrine entrance. In {{ill|lt=Meiji 4|Q11512280|qid=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}} ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}), Mumino Shrine, whose location had been unknown since the medieval period, was enshrined together with Mifune Shrine. === Access === ;Public transportation * About a 17-minute walk (approximately 900 m) from {{ill|lt=Tokida Station|Q7813584|qid=Q7813584|1=Tokida Station}} on the {{ill|lt=JR|Q513679|qid=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sangū Line|Q872023|qid=Q872023|1=Sangū Line}}.Ise Bunka-sha, ed. (2008): p.63 Walk along the {{ill|lt=Miyagawa Irrigation Canal|Q11454721|qid=Q11454721|1=Miyagawa Irrigation Canal}} from the front (south) side of Tokida Station; the elevated ground visible on the right is Mifune Shrine / Mumino Shrine. ;By car * About 14 minutes (approximately 5.9 {{ill|lt=kilometers|Q828224|qid=Q828224|lt=km|1=Kilometre}}) from {{ill|lt=Tamaki Interchange|Q11572465|qid=Q11572465|lt=Tamaki IC|1=Tamaki interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Ise Expressway|Q6079578|qid=Q6079578|1=Ise Expressway}}. There is no {{ill|lt=parking lot|Q6501349|qid=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} nearby. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{ill|lt=Ise Bunka-sha|Q11379080|qid=Q11379080|1=Ise
